<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" ALIGN="right"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">(1.8</FONT></TD>
<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">)%&nbsp;</FONT></TD></TR>
</TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">For the fourth quarter of 2014, we reported a net income of $43&nbsp;million, compared to
a net loss of $36 in the prior-year quarter and a net income of $72 million in the prior quarter. The fourth quarter net income represented earnings per share of $0.05 compared to $(0.04) in the prior-year quarter and $0.08 in the prior quarter.
</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">In the fourth quarter of 2014, the impact per share after tax of impairment, restructuring charges and other
related closure costs and other <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">one-time</FONT> items, a non U.S.&nbsp;GAAP measure, was approximately $(0.02) per share, while it was approximately $(0.03) and $(0.05)&nbsp;per share in the prior-year and prior
quarters, respectively. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B>Impact of Changes in Exchange Rates </B></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Our results of operations and financial condition can be significantly affected by material changes in the exchange rates
between the U.S.&nbsp;dollar and other currencies, particularly the Euro. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">As a market rule, the reference
currency for the semiconductor industry is the U.S.&nbsp;dollar and the market prices of semiconductor products are mainly denominated in U.S.&nbsp;dollars. However revenues for some of our products (primarily certain of our products sold in Europe)
are quoted in currencies other than the U.S. dollar and as such are directly affected by fluctuations in the value of the U.S. dollar. As a result of currency variations, the appreciation of the Euro compared to the U.S. dollar could increase our
level of revenues when reported in U.S. dollars or the depreciation of the Euro compared to the U.S. dollar could decrease our level of revenues when reported in U.S. dollars. Over time the prices in the industry tend to align to the equivalent
amount in U.S. dollars, except that there is a lag between the changes in the currency rate and the adjustment in the price paid in local currency, which is proportional to the amplitude of the currency swing, and such adjustment could be only
partial. Furthermore, certain significant costs incurred by us, such as manufacturing costs, SG&amp;A expenses, and R&amp;D expenses, are largely incurred in the currency of the jurisdictions in which our operations are located. Given that most of
our operations are located in the Euro zone and other non U.S.&nbsp;dollar currency areas, including Singapore, our costs tend to increase when translated into U.S.&nbsp;dollars when the dollar weakens or to decrease when the U.S.&nbsp;dollar
strengthens. </FONT></P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="center"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">54 </FONT></P>



<p Style='page-break-before:always'>
<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">In summary, as our reporting currency is the U.S.&nbsp;dollar, exchange rate
fluctuations affect our results of operations: in particular, if the U.S.&nbsp;dollar weakens, our results are negatively impacted since we receive only a limited part of our revenues, and more importantly, we incur a significant part of our costs,
in currencies other than the U.S.&nbsp;dollar. On the other hand, our results are favorably impacted when the dollar strengthens. The impact on our accounts could therefore be material, in the case of a material variation of the U.S.&nbsp;dollar
exchange rate. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Our principal strategy to reduce the risks associated with exchange rate fluctuations has been
to balance as much as possible the proportion of sales to our customers denominated in U.S.&nbsp;dollars with the amount of materials, purchases and services from our suppliers denominated in U.S.&nbsp;dollars, thereby reducing the potential
exchange rate impact of certain variable costs relative to revenues. Moreover, in order to further reduce the exposure to U.S.&nbsp;dollar exchange fluctuations, we have hedged certain line items on our Consolidated Statements of Income, in
particular with respect to a portion of the costs of goods sold, most of the R&amp;D&nbsp;expenses and certain SG&amp;A expenses, located in the Euro zone, which we account for as cash flow hedging contracts. We use three&nbsp;different types of
hedging contracts, consisting of forward contracts, collars and options. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Our Consolidated Statements of
Income for 2014 included income and expense items translated at the average U.S.&nbsp;dollar exchange rate for the period, plus the impact of the hedging contracts expiring during the period. Our effective average exchange rate was $1.34 for
&#128;1.00 for the full year 2014, compared to $1.31 for &#128;1.00 for the full year 2013. Our effective exchange rate was $1.29 for &#128;1.00 for the fourth quarter of 2014, $1.34 for &#128;1.00 for the third quarter of 2014 and $1.34 for
&#128;1.00 for the fourth quarter of 2013. These effective exchange rates reflect the actual exchange rates combined with the impact of cash flow hedging contracts that matured in the period. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">The time horizon of our cash flow hedging for manufacturing costs and operating expenses may run up to 24&nbsp;months,
for a limited percentage of our exposure to the Euro and under certain currency market circumstances. As of December&nbsp;31, 2014, the outstanding hedged amounts were &#128;625&nbsp;million to cover manufacturing costs and &#128;432&nbsp;million to
cover operating expenses, at an average exchange rate of about $1.32 to &#128;1.00 (considering the collars at upper strike), maturing over the period from January&nbsp;5, 2015 to December&nbsp;3, 2015. As of December&nbsp;31, 2014, these
outstanding hedging contracts and certain expiring contracts covering manufacturing expenses capitalized in inventory resulted in a deferred loss of approximately $73&nbsp;million before tax, recorded in &#147;Accumulated other comprehensive income
(loss)&#148; in the Consolidated Statements of Equity, compared to a deferred profit of approximately $39&nbsp;million before tax at December&nbsp;31, 2013. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">We also hedge certain manufacturing costs denominated in Singapore dollars (SGD); as of December&nbsp;31, 2014, the outstanding hedged amounts were SGD 136&nbsp;million at an average exchange rate of
about SGD 1.28 to $1.00 maturing over the period from January&nbsp;8, 2015 to December&nbsp;4, 2015. As of December&nbsp;31, 2014, these outstanding hedging contracts resulted in a deferred loss of approximately $3 million before tax, recorded in
&#147;Accumulated other comprehensive income (loss)&#148; in the Consolidated Statements of Equity, compared to a deferred loss of approximately $1 million before tax at December&nbsp;31, 2013. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Our cash flow hedging policy is not intended to cover our full exposure and is based on hedging a portion of our exposure
in the next four quarters and a declining percentage of our exposure in each quarter thereafter. In 2014, as a result of our cash flow hedging, we recorded a net loss of $2&nbsp;million, consisting of a loss of about $1 million to selling, general
and administrative expenses and a loss of about $1&nbsp;million to costs of goods sold, while in 2013, we recorded a net profit of $33&nbsp;million. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">In addition to our cash flow hedging, in order to mitigate potential exchange rate risks on our commercial transactions, we purchase and enter into forward foreign currency exchange contracts and currency
options to cover foreign currency exposure in payables or receivables at our affiliates, which we account for as fair value instruments. We may in the future purchase or sell similar types of instruments. See &#147;Item&nbsp;11. Quantitative and
Qualitative Disclosures About Market Risk&#148;. Furthermore, we may not predict in a timely fashion the amount of future transactions in the volatile industry environment. No assurance may be given that our hedging activities will sufficiently
protect us against declines in the value of the U.S.&nbsp;dollar. Consequently, our results of operations have been and may continue to be impacted by fluctuations in exchange rates. The net effect of our consolidated foreign exchange exposure
resulted in a net gain of $4&nbsp;million recorded in &#147;Other income and expenses, net&#148; in our 2014 Consolidated Statement of Income compared to a net gain of $8&nbsp;million recorded in 2013. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">The assets and liabilities of subsidiaries are, for consolidation purposes, translated into U.S.&nbsp;dollars at the <FONT
STYLE="white-space:nowrap">period-end</FONT> exchange rate. Income and expenses, as well as cash flows, are translated at the average exchange rate for the period. The balance sheet impact, as well as the income statement and cash flow impact, of
such translations have been, and may be expected to be, significant from period to period since a large part of our assets and liabilities and activities are accounted for in Euros as they are located in jurisdictions where the Euro
</FONT></P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="center"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">55 </FONT></P>



<p Style='page-break-before:always'>
<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">
is the functional currency. Adjustments resulting from the translation are recorded directly in equity, and are shown as &#147;Accumulated other comprehensive income (loss)&#148; in the
Consolidated Statements of Equity. At December&nbsp;31, 2014, our outstanding indebtedness was denominated mainly in U.S.&nbsp;dollars and in Euros. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">For a more detailed discussion, see &#147;Item&nbsp;3. Key Information &#151; Risk Factors &#151; Risks Related to Our Operations&#148;. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B>Impact of Changes in Interest Rates </B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Interest rates may fluctuate upon changes in financial market conditions and material changes can affect our results of operations and financial condition, since these changes can impact the total
interest income received on our cash and cash equivalents and marketable securities, as well as the total interest expense paid on our financial debt. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Our interest income (expense), net, as reported in our Consolidated Statements of Income, is the balance between interest income received from our cash and cash equivalents and marketable securities
investments and interest expense paid on our financial liabilities (including the sale without recourse of receivables), non-cash interest expense on the Senior Bonds and bank fees (including fees on committed credit lines). Our interest income is
dependent upon fluctuations in interest rates, mainly in U.S. dollars and Euros, since we invest primarily on a <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">short-term</FONT> basis; any increase or decrease in the market interest rates would mean an equivalent
increase or decrease in our interest income. Our interest expenses are also dependent upon fluctuations in interest rates, since our financial liabilities include European Investment Bank Floating Rate Loans at Libor and Euribor plus variable
spreads. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">At December&nbsp;31, 2014, our total financial resources, including cash and cash equivalents and
marketable securities, generated an average interest income rate of 0.63%. At the same date, the average interest rate on our outstanding debt was 1.83% while the average rate of the cash interests on our total debt at redemption value was 0.67%.
</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B>Impact of Changes in Equity Prices </B></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">As of December&nbsp;31, 2014, we did not hold any significant equity participations, which could be subject to a material
impact in changes in equity prices. However, we hold equity participations whose carrying value could be reduced due to further losses or impairment charges of our <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">equity-method</FONT> investments. See Note&nbsp;10
to our Consolidated Financial Statements. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B>Liquidity and Capital Resources </B></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Treasury activities are regulated by our policies, which define procedures, objectives and controls. The policies focus on
the management of our financial risk in terms of exposure to currency rates and interest rates. Most treasury activities are centralized, with any local treasury activities subject to oversight from our head treasury office. The majority of our cash
and cash equivalents are held in U.S.&nbsp;dollars and Euros and are placed with financial institutions rated at least a single A <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">long-term</FONT> rating, meaning at least A3 from Moody&#146;s Investors Service
(&#147;Moody&#146;s&#148;) and <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">A-</FONT> from Standard&nbsp;&amp;&nbsp;Poor&#146;s (&#147;S&amp;P&#148;) or Fitch Ratings (&#147;Fitch&#148;), or better. Marginal amounts are held in other currencies. See
&#147;Item&nbsp;11. Quantitative and Qualitative Disclosures About Market Risk&#148;. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Our total liquidity and
capital resources were $2,351&nbsp;million as of December&nbsp;31, 2014, increasing compared to $1,894&nbsp;million at December&nbsp;31, 2013. As of December&nbsp;31, 2014, our total liquidity and capital resources were comprised of
$2,017&nbsp;million in cash and cash equivalents and $334&nbsp;million in marketable securities, all considered as current assets. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">As of December&nbsp;31, 2014, marketable securities were $334&nbsp;million invested in U.S. Government Treasury Bonds with an average rating of Aaa/AA+/AAA from Moody&#146;s, S&amp;P&nbsp;and Fitch,
respectively. The weighted average maturity of the marketable securities portfolio was 5.3&nbsp;years. The securities are classified as <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap"><FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">available-for-sale</FONT></FONT> and reported
at fair value. This fair value measurement corresponds to a Level 1 fair value hierarchy measurement. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B>Liquidity
</B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">We maintain a significant cash position and a low <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap"><FONT
STYLE="white-space:nowrap">debt-to-equity</FONT></FONT> ratio, which provide us with adequate financial flexibility. As in the past, our cash management policy is to finance our investment needs mainly with net cash generated from operating
activities. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">During 2014, our net cash increased by $181&nbsp;million, due to the net cash from operating and
financing activities exceeding the net cash used in investing activities. </FONT></P>

</TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><I>Net cash from operating activities</I>. Net cash from operating activities is the sum
of (i)&nbsp;net income (loss) adjusted for <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">non-cash</FONT> items and (ii)&nbsp;changes in net working capital. The net cash from operating activities in 2014 was $715&nbsp;million, increasing compared to
$366&nbsp;million in the prior&nbsp;year period. Net cash from operating activities for 2014 compared to the prior year benefited from an increased net income adjusted for non-cash items, partially offset by the unfavorable changes in net working
capital. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><I>Net cash used in investing activities</I>. Investing activities used $784&nbsp;million of cash in
2014, mainly due to payments for the purchase of tangible, intangible, financial assets and marketable securities, partially offset by the proceeds from the sale of marketable securities and the sale of businesses. The increase in net cash used in
investing activities compared to the $379 million in the prior year was primarily due to a higher payment for purchase of marketable securities, net of proceeds, a lower amount of proceeds from the sale of businesses and a lower amount of of
payments for purchase of tangible assets, net of proceeds. Payments for purchase of tangible assets, net of proceeds, totaled $496&nbsp;million, compared to $531&nbsp;million in 2013. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><I>Net cash from</I> (<I>used in</I>)<I> financing activities</I>. Net cash from financing activities was $262 million
for 2014, compared to the $388 million used in 2013. The increase in the net cash from financing activities was primarily due to the $994 million net proceeds from the issuance of the Senior Bonds in 2014, partially offset by $156 million of
repurchases of common stock. The 2014 amount included $354 million in dividends paid to stockholders compared to $346 million paid in 2013. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><I>Free Cash Flow</I> (<I>non U</I>.<I>S</I>. <I>GAAP measure</I>). We also present Free Cash Flow, which is a non U.S.&nbsp;GAAP measure, defined as (i)&nbsp;net cash from operating activities plus
(ii)&nbsp;net cash used in investing activities, excluding payment for purchases (and proceeds from the sale) of marketable securities, which are considered as temporary financial investments. The result of this definition is ultimately net cash
from operating activities plus payment for purchase and proceeds from sale of tangible, intangible and financial assets and proceeds received in the sale of businesses. We believe Free Cash Flow, a non U.S.&nbsp;GAAP measure, provides useful
information for investors and management because it measures our capacity to generate cash from our operating and investing activities to sustain our operations. Free Cash Flow is not a U.S.&nbsp;GAAP measure and does not represent total cash flow
since it does not include the cash flows generated by or used in financing activities. Free Cash Flow reconciles with the total cash flow and the net cash increase (decrease) by including the payment for purchases (and proceeds from the sale) of
marketable securities and net cash variation from joint ventures deconsolidation, the net cash from (used in) financing activities and the effect of changes in exchange rates. In addition, our definition of Free Cash Flow may differ from definitions
used by other companies. Free Cash Flow is determined as follows from our Consolidated Statements of Cash Flows: </FONT></P> <P STYLE="font-size:12px;margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px">&nbsp;</P>

<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Free Cash Flow was positive $197 million in 2014, compared to negative $179
million in 2013, reflecting the exit from ST-Ericsson, our performance improvement, monetization of non strategic assets and collection of a higher level of R&amp;D grants including a catch-up of prior year grants. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B>Capital Resources </B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><I>Net Financial Position</I> (<I>non U</I>.<I>S</I>. <I>GAAP measure</I>). Our Net Financial Position represents the difference between our total financial resources and our total financial debt. Our
total financial resources include cash and cash equivalents, marketable securities, <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">short-term</FONT> deposits and restricted cash, and our total financial debt includes bank overdrafts, <FONT
STYLE="white-space:nowrap">short-term</FONT> debt and <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">long-term</FONT> debt, as represented in our Consolidated Balance Sheets. Net Financial Position is not a U.S.&nbsp;GAAP measure but we believe it provides useful
information for investors because it gives evidence of our global position either in terms of net indebtedness or net cash by measuring our capital resources based on cash and cash equivalents and marketable securities and the total level of our
financial indebtedness. Our Net Financial Position for each period has been determined as follows from our Consolidated Balance Sheets: </FONT></P> <P STYLE="font-size:12px;margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px">&nbsp;</P>

</TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Our Net Financial Position as of December&nbsp;31, 2014 was a net cash position of
$546&nbsp;million, decreasing compared to the net cash position of $741&nbsp;million at December&nbsp;31, 2013, as a result of our dividends payment, the repurchase of common stock and the debt resulting from the issuance of the Senior Bonds
exceeding our positive Free Cash Flow and the net proceeds from the issuance of the Senior Bonds. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">At
December&nbsp;31, 2014, our financial debt was $1,805&nbsp;million, composed of (i)&nbsp;$202&nbsp;million of current portion of <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">long-term</FONT> debt and (ii)&nbsp;$1,603&nbsp;million of <FONT
STYLE="white-space:nowrap">long-term</FONT> debt. The breakdown of our total financial debt included: (i)&nbsp;$900&nbsp;million in European Investment Bank loans (the &#147;EIB Loans&#148;), (ii)&nbsp;$888 million in the Senior Bonds,
(iii)&nbsp;$16&nbsp;million in loans from other funding programs, and (iv)&nbsp;$1 million of capital leases. The EIB Loans are comprised of <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">four&nbsp;long-term</FONT> amortizing credit facilities as part of our
R&amp;D funding programs. The first for R&amp;D in France was drawn in U.S.&nbsp;dollars from 2006 to 2008 for a total amount of $341&nbsp;million, of which $48&nbsp;million remained outstanding as at December&nbsp;31, 2014. The second for R&amp;D
projects in Italy, was drawn in U.S.&nbsp;dollars in 2008&nbsp;for a total amount of $380&nbsp;million, of which $109&nbsp;million remained outstanding as of December&nbsp;31, 2014. The third, signed in 2010, is a &#128;350&nbsp;million <FONT
STYLE="white-space:nowrap">multi-currency</FONT> loan to support our industrial and R&amp;D programs. It was drawn mainly in U.S.&nbsp;dollars for an amount of $321&nbsp;million and only partially in Euros for an amount of &#128;100&nbsp;million, of
which $331&nbsp;million remained outstanding as of December&nbsp;31, 2014. The fourth, signed in the first quarter of 2013, is a &#128;350&nbsp;million multicurrency loan which also supports our R&amp;D programs. It was drawn in U.S.&nbsp;dollars
for an amount of $471&nbsp;million, of which $412 million is outstanding as of December&nbsp;31, 2014. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Additionally, we had unutilized committed <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">medium-term</FONT> credit facilities with core
relationship banks of $583&nbsp;million. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Our <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">long-term</FONT> debt contains
standard conditions, but does not impose minimum financial ratios. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">As of December&nbsp;31, 2014, debt
payments at redemption value by period were as follows: </FONT></P> <P STYLE="font-size:12px;margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px">&nbsp;</P>

 <p STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="center"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">58 </FONT></P>



<p Style='page-break-before:always'>
<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">The Senior Bonds were issued on July&nbsp;3, 2014, for a principal amount of
$1,000 million (Tranche A for $600 million and Tranche B for $400 million), due 2019 and 2021, respectively, for net proceeds of approximately $994 million. Tranche A bonds were issued as zero-coupon bonds while Tranche B bonds bear a 1%&nbsp;per
annum nominal interest, payable semi-annually. The conversion price at issuance was approximately $12 on each tranche. The Senior Bonds are convertible by the bondholders if certain conditions are satisfied on a net-share settlement basis, except if
an alternative settlement is elected by us. We can also redeem the Senior Bonds prior to their maturity in certain circumstances. Upon initial recognition, the proceeds were allocated between debt and equity by determining the fair value of the
liability component using an income approach. The liability component will accrete to par value until maturity based on the effective interest rate (Tranche A: 2.40% and Tranche B: 3.22%, including 1% p.a. nominal interest). In the computation of
diluted earnings per share, the Senior Bonds will be dilutive only for the portion of net-share settlement underlying the conversion premium when the conversion option is in the money. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">In March 2006, STMicroelectronics Finance B.V. (&#147;ST BV&#148;), a wholly owned subsidiary, issued floating rate
senior bonds. These bonds, which matured on March&nbsp;17, 2013, paid a quarterly coupon rate of the <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">three-month</FONT> Euribor plus 0.40%. On March&nbsp;17, 2013, we repaid at maturity with available cash the
residual outstanding bonds in the amount of $455 million. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">On June&nbsp;26, 2014 we announced the launch of a
share buy-back program for the purchase of up to 20&nbsp;million ordinary shares, as authorized by the shareholders&#146; meeting held on June&nbsp;13, 2014. Purchases of shares were made on the Borsa Italiana exclusively and were completed on
November&nbsp;10, 2014. The purchased shares will not be retired but are currently intended to meet our future obligations in relation to the employee stock award plans. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Our current rating with rating agencies are as follow: Moody&#146;s: &#147;Baa3&#148; with stable outlook; S&amp;P:
&#147;BBB-&#148; with stable outlook; Fitch (on an unsolicited basis): &#147;BBB-&#148; with stable outlook. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><I>Contractual
Obligations, Commercial Commitments and Contingencies </I></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%;padding-bottom:0px; " ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Our contractual obligations,
commercial commitments and contingencies as of December&nbsp;31, 2014, and for each of the five&nbsp;years to come and thereafter, were as follows:</FONT><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="1"><SUP
STYLE="vertical-align:baseline; position:relative; bottom:.8ex">(1)</SUP></FONT><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"> </FONT></P> <P STYLE="font-size:12px;margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px">&nbsp;</P>

of certain manufacturing facilities, some of the aforementioned contracts have been terminated. The termination fees for the sites still in operation have not been taken into account. </FONT></P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="center"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">59 </FONT></P>



<p Style='page-break-before:always'>
<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Operating leases are mainly related to building leases and to equipment. The
amount disclosed is composed of minimum payments for future leases from 2015 to 2019 and thereafter. We lease land, buildings, plants and equipment under operating leases that expire at various dates under
<FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">non-cancelable</FONT> lease agreements. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Purchase obligations are primarily
comprised of purchase commitments for equipment, for outsourced foundry wafers and for software licenses. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Other obligations primarily relate to firm contractual commitments with respect to partnership and cooperation
agreements. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">Long-term</FONT> debt obligations mainly consist of bank loans
and Senior Bonds. In 2015, we expect to redeem with available cash and cash equivalents a $202&nbsp;million loan received from European Investment Bank as an annual installment. See &#147;&#151; Net financial position (non U.S.&nbsp;GAAP
measure)&#148; above. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Pension obligations amounting to $392&nbsp;million consist of our best estimates of the
amounts projected to be payable by us for the pension and <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">post-employment</FONT> plans. The final actual amount to be paid and related timing of such payments may vary significantly due to early retirements,
terminations and changes in assumptions rates. See Note 14 to our Consolidated Financial Statements. As part of the Flash divestiture, we retained the obligation to fund the severance payment (<I>trattamento di fine rapporto</I>) due to certain
transferred employees by the defined amount of about $10&nbsp;million which qualifies as a defined benefit plan and was classified as an &#147;other <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">long-term</FONT> liability&#148; at December&nbsp;31, 2014.
</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Other <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">long-term</FONT> liabilities include future obligations related to our
restructuring plans and miscellaneous contractual obligations. In accordance with the authoritative guidance for accounting for uncertainty in income taxes, as of December&nbsp;31, 2014, we had unrecognized tax benefits of $313&nbsp;million. We do
not expect to recognize any of these tax benefits in 2015. We are not, however, able to provide a reasonably reliable estimate of when these benefits will be recognized. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><I><FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">Off-Balance</FONT> Sheet Arrangements </I></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">We had no material <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">off-balance</FONT> sheet arrangements at December&nbsp;31, 2014. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B>Financial Outlook: Capital Investment </B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Our policy is to
modulate our capital spending according to the evolution of the semiconductor market. Based on current visibility on demand, we anticipate our capital expenditure to be approximately $600 million in 2015, to be adjusted based on demand thereafter.
The most important of our 2015 capital expenditure projects are expected to be: (a)&nbsp;for our <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">front-end</FONT> facilities: (i)&nbsp;in our <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">300-mm</FONT> fab in Crolles, technology
evolution and mix evolution to support the production ramp up of new technologies and start-up of the infrastructure for its next expansion phase; (ii)&nbsp;a few selective programs of mix evolution, mainly in the area of analog processes;
(iii)&nbsp;qualification and ramp-up of technologies in <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">200-mm</FONT> in Singapore and Catania and start-up of the infrastructure for the next expansion phase in Catania; and (iv)&nbsp;quality, safety, maintenance,
and productivity and cost savings investments in both <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">150-mm</FONT> and <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">200-mm</FONT> <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">front-end</FONT> fabs; (b)&nbsp;for our <FONT
STYLE="white-space:nowrap">back-end</FONT> facilities, capital expenditures will mainly be dedicated to: (i)&nbsp;capacity growth on certain package families, to sustain market demand and secure service to strategic customers;
(ii)&nbsp;modernization and rationalization of package lines targeting cost savings benefits; and (iii)&nbsp;specific investments in the areas of factory automation, quality, environment and energy savings; and (c)&nbsp;an overall capacity
adjustment in final testing and wafers probing (EWS) to meet increased demand and changed product mix. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">We
will continue to monitor our level of capital spending by taking into consideration factors such as trends in the semiconductor industry and capacity utilization. We expect to need significant financial resources in the coming&nbsp;years for capital
expenditures and for our investments in manufacturing and R&amp;D. We plan to fund our capital requirements from cash provided by operating activities, available funds and support from third&nbsp;parties, and may have recourse to borrowings under
available credit lines and, to the extent necessary or attractive based on market conditions prevailing at the time, the issuance of debt, convertible bonds or additional equity securities. A substantial deterioration of our economic results, and
consequently of our profitability, could generate a deterioration of the cash generated by our operating activities. Therefore, there can be no assurance that, in future periods, we will generate the same level of cash as in prior&nbsp;years to fund
our capital expenditure plans for expanding/upgrading our production facilities, our working capital requirements, our R&amp;D and manufacturing costs. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="font-size:12px;margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="center"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">60 </FONT></P>



<p Style='page-break-before:always'>
<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">In support of our R&amp;D activities, we signed the Nano2017 program with
the French government in 2013, which was approved by the European Union in the second quarter of 2014 and, in our role as Coordinator and Project Leader of Nano2017, we have been allocated an overall funding budget of about &#128;400&nbsp;million
for the period 2013-2017, subject to the conclusion of agreements every year with the public authorities and linked to the achievement of technical parameters and objectives. The Nano2017 contract contains certain covenants which, in the event they
are not fulfilled, may affect our ability to access such funding. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">As a result of our exit from the <FONT
STYLE="white-space:nowrap">ST-Ericsson</FONT> joint venture, our exposure is limited to covering 50% of <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">ST-Ericsson&#146;s</FONT> needs to complete the <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">wind-down,</FONT> which are
estimated to be not material to our consolidated cash flows, based on our current visibility of the ST-Ericsson liquidation balance. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">We believe that we have the financial resources needed to meet our currently projected business requirements for the next twelve&nbsp;months, including capital expenditures for our manufacturing
activities, working capital requirements, approved dividend payments and the repayment of our debts in line with their maturity dates. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B>Impact of Recently Issued U.S. Accounting Standards </B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">See
Note 2 to our Consolidated Financial Statements. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B><FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">Equity-method</FONT> investments
</B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">See Note 10 to our Consolidated Financial Statements. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B>Backlog and Customers </B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">See &#147;Item&nbsp;4. Information on the Company &#151; Backlog&#148;. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="font-size:18px;margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px">&nbsp;</P>

<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top"> <P ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B>Directors, Senior Management and Employees </B></FONT></P></TD></TR></TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B>Directors and Senior Management </B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">The management of our
Company is entrusted to the Managing Board under the supervision of the Supervisory Board. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B><I>Supervisory Board
</I></B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Our Supervisory Board advises our Managing Board and is responsible for supervising the policies
pursued by our Managing Board and the general course of our affairs and business. Our Supervisory Board consists of such number of members as is resolved by our Annual General Meeting of Shareholders (&#147;AGM&#148;) upon a <FONT
STYLE="white-space:nowrap">non-binding</FONT> proposal of our Supervisory Board, with a minimum of six members. Decisions by our AGM concerning the number and the identity of our Supervisory Board members are taken by a simple majority of the votes
cast at a meeting, provided quorum conditions are met. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Our Supervisory Board was composed of the following
nine members as of December&nbsp;31, 2014: </FONT></P> <P STYLE="font-size:12px;margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px">&nbsp;</P>

 <p STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="center"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">61 </FONT></P>

<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Resolutions of our Supervisory Board require the approval of at least
three&nbsp;quarters of its members in office. Our Supervisory Board must meet upon request by two&nbsp;or more of its members or by our Managing Board. Our Supervisory Board meets at least five times a&nbsp;year, including once per quarter to
approve our quarterly, semi-annual and annual accounts and their release. Our Supervisory Board has adopted a Supervisory Board Charter setting forth its duties, responsibilities and operations, as mentioned below. This charter is available on our
website (www.st.com). </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Pursuant to Dutch law, there is no mandatory retirement age for members of our
Supervisory Board. Members of the Supervisory Board may be suspended or dismissed by our annual shareholders&#146; meeting. Our Supervisory Board may make a proposal to our annual shareholders&#146; meeting for the suspension or dismissal of one or
more of its members. Each member of our Supervisory Board must resign no later than three&nbsp;years after appointment, as described in our Articles of Association, but may be reappointed following the expiration of his/her term of office.
</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><I>Biographies of our Current Supervisory Board Members </I></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Maurizio Tamagnini has been a member and the Chairman of our Supervisory Board since June 2014. He also serves on our
Supervisory Board&#146;s Nominating and Corporate Governance Committee and chairs its Compensation Committee and Strategic Committee. Mr.&nbsp;Tamagnini is currently Chief Executive Officer and Chairman of the Investment Committee of Fondo
Strategico Italiano Spa (FSI), an investment company with &#128;4.4 billion capital which invests in large corporates, sponsored by Cassa depositi e prestiti Spa. He was previously Southern European Manager of the Corporate&nbsp;&amp; Investments
Banking division of Bank of America Merrill Lynch and a member of the Executive Committee of Bank of America Merrill Lynch for the EMEA region. Mr.&nbsp;Tamagnini has gained over 25&nbsp;years of experience in the financial sector specializing in
the areas of Corporate Finance, Private Equity, Debt and Equity. Mr.&nbsp;Tamagnini is also Chairman of the Joint Venture between FSI and Qatar Holding (IQ Made in Italy Investment Company Spa) with capital endowment of up to &#128;2 billion in
total for investments in the food, brands, furniture&nbsp;&amp; design and tourism sectors. He is also a member of the Advisory Board of RDIF (the Russian Direct Investment Fund), of the Italian Ministry of Economic Development Industrial Compact
task force and was a member of the Organization Committee for the 2014 Worldwide Female Volleyball Championships, held in Milan. He holds a degree in International Monetary Economics from Bocconi University in Milan and has also studied at the
Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute &#151; Troy in New York, USA. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Didier Lombard has been a member of our
Supervisory Board since 2004 and has been its <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">Vice-Chairman</FONT> since June&nbsp;2014. He was the Supervisory Board&#146;s Chairman from 2011 until 2014. Mr.&nbsp;Lombard serves on our Supervisory Board&#146;s
Compensation Committee, Strategic Committee and Nominating and Corporate Governance Committee. Mr.&nbsp;Lombard was appointed Chairman and Chief Executive Officer of Orange (formerly France Telecom) in March&nbsp;2005, and served as Chief Executive
Officer until February&nbsp;2010 and Chairman until March&nbsp;2011. Mr.&nbsp;Lombard began his career in the Research and Development division of Orange in 1967. From 1989 to 1990, he served as scientific and technological director at the Ministry
of Research and Technology. From 1991 to 1998, he served as General Director for industrial strategies at the French Ministry of Economy, Finances and Industry, and from 1999 to 2003 he served as an Ambassador at large for foreign investments in
France and as President of the French Agency for International Investments. From 2003 through February&nbsp;2005, he served as Orange&#146;s Senior Executive Vice President in charge of technologies, strategic partnerships and new usages and as a
member of Orange&#146;s Executive Committee. Mr.&nbsp;Lombard was also a member of the board of directors of Thales until May 2014. He is also the Chairman of the board of directors of Technicolor (previously Thomson), one of our customers, as well
as a member of the supervisory board of Radiall. Mr.&nbsp;Lombard was also a member until his resignation on November&nbsp;15, 2006 of the supervisory board of ST Holding, our largest shareholder. Mr.&nbsp;Lombard is a graduate of the Ecole
Polytechnique and the Ecole Nationale Sup&eacute;rieure des T&eacute;l&eacute;communications. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Jean
d&#146;Arthuys has been a member of our Supervisory Board since May&nbsp;2011. Mr.&nbsp;d&#146;Arthuys serves on our Supervisory Board&#146;s Compensation Committee, Strategic Committee and Nominating and Corporate Governance Committee.
Mr.&nbsp;d&#146;Arthuys is also the Chairman and CEO of FT1CI. He joined Bpifrance (formerly Fonds Strat&eacute;gique d&#146;Investissement) in 2010 as Director and member of the Executive Committee. Mr.&nbsp;d&#146;Arthuys was a partner in the fund
PAI Partners from 2007 until 2010, in particular in charge of the sectors media, internet and telecom. He was previously Chairman and Chief Executive Officer of television channels Paris Premiere and W9. Mr.&nbsp;d&#146;Arthuys spent the main part
of his career at the Executive Board of the Group M6, where he had various functions (from 1996 until 2007). He managed in particular the activities of digital television and the development of the Group. He was a board member of TPS, Sportfive and
Newsweb. Mr.&nbsp;d&#146;Arthuys was also Chairman and Chief Executive Officer of the soccer club Girondins de Bordeaux. Mr. d&#146;Arthuys is also a member of the board of directors of Eutelsat Communications S.A. and Talend Inc.
Mr.&nbsp;d&#146;Arthuys graduated from HEC Business School. </FONT></P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="center"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">62 </FONT></P>



<p Style='page-break-before:always'>

<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Janet G. Davidson has been a member of our Supervisory Board since
June&nbsp;2013. She serves on our Supervisory Board&#146;s Audit Committee and Strategic Committee. She began her career in 1979 as a member of the Technical Staff of Bell Laboratories, Lucent Technologies (as of 2006 Alcatel Lucent), and served
from 1979 through 2011 in several key positions, most recently as Chief Strategy Officer (2005 &#150; 2006), Chief Compliance Officer (2006 &#150; 2008) and EVP Quality&nbsp;&amp;&nbsp;Customer Care (2008 &#150; 2011). From 2005 through 2012,
Ms.&nbsp;Davidson was a member of the Lehigh University Board of Trustees. In 2007 she served on the Riverside Symphonia Board of Trustees and in 2005 and 2006, Ms.&nbsp;Davidson was a member of the Liberty Science Center Board of Trustees.
Ms.&nbsp;Davidson was a member of the board of the Alcatel Lucent Foundation from 2011 until 2014. Ms.&nbsp;Davidson is a graduate of the Georgia Institute of Technology (Georgia Tech), Atlanta, GA, USA, and Lehigh University, Bethlehem, PA, USA and
holds a Master&#146;s degree in Electrical Engineering. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Heleen Kersten has been a member of our Supervisory
Board since June 2014. She serves on our Supervisory Board&#146;s Audit Committee and Compensation Committee and chairs its Nominating and Corporate Governance Committee. Ms.&nbsp;Kersten is a partner at Stibbe in Amsterdam, where she held the
position of managing partner from 2008 to 2013. Stibbe is a Benelux law firm with offices in Amsterdam, Brussels, Luxembourg, London, New York, Dubai and Hong Kong. She began her career in 1989 with Stibbe before joining Davis Polk in New York and
London <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">(1992-1993).</FONT> After her return to Stibbe Amsterdam, she rose through the ranks to become a partner in 1997. As a member of the Bar of Amsterdam since 1989, Ms.&nbsp;Kersten specializes in mergers and
acquisitions, equity capital markets, corporate law and corporate governance. Ms.&nbsp;Kersten is currently a member of the supervisory boards of Egeria Investment B.V. (since 2007 and Chairman since October 2014) and Van Lanschot N.V. (since 2011).
Ms.&nbsp;Kersten holds master&#146;s degrees in Dutch law and tax law, both from Leiden University in the Netherlands. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">Jean-Georges</FONT> Malcor has been a member of our Supervisory Board since May&nbsp;2011. He also serves on our Supervisory Board&#146;s Audit Committee. Mr.&nbsp;Malcor
is the Chief Executive Officer of CGG. He is a graduate of Ecole Centrale de Paris. He also holds a Master of Sciences degree from Stanford University, and a Doctorat from Ecole des Mines. Mr.&nbsp;Malcor began his career at the Thales group as an
acoustic engineer in the Underwater Activities division where he was particularly in charge of hydrophone and geophone design and towed streamer programs. He then moved to the Sydney based Thomson Sintra Pacific Australia, becoming Managing Director
of the company in 1990. Back in France, he became Director of Marketing and Communications (1991), then Director, Foreign Operations of Thomson Sintra Activit&eacute;s Sous Marines (1993). In 1996, he was appointed Managing Director of Thomson
Marconi Sonar Australia which was, in addition to its military activities, the lead developing company for the solid geophysical streamer. In 1999, Mr.&nbsp;Malcor became the first Managing Director of the newly formed joint venture Australian
Defense Industry. During this time he operated the Sydney based Woolloomooloo Shipyard (the largest dry dock in the southern hemisphere). In 2002, he became Senior Vice President, International Operations of Thales International. From 2004 to 2009,
he was Senior Vice President in charge of the Naval Division, supervising all naval activities in Thales including ship design, building and maintenance. In January&nbsp;2009, he became Senior Vice President, in charge of the Aerospace Division. In
June&nbsp;2009, he moved to the position of Senior Vice President, Continental Europe, Turkey, Russia, Asia, Africa, Middle East, and Latin America. Mr.&nbsp;Malcor joined CGG in January&nbsp;2010 as President and became CEO on June&nbsp;30, 2010.
Since June&nbsp;2013, Mr.&nbsp;Malcor has been a member of the Supervisory Board (as well as its Appointment and Compensation Committee) of the Fives Group. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Alessandro Ovi was a member of our Supervisory Board from 1994 until his term expired at our Annual General Meeting of Shareholders in March&nbsp;2005. He was reappointed to our Supervisory Board at the
2007 Annual General Meeting of Shareholders. Mr.&nbsp;Ovi serves on our Supervisory Board&#146;s Audit Committee and Strategic Committee. Mr.&nbsp;Ovi received a doctoral degree in Nuclear Engineering from the Politecnico in Milan and a
Master&#146;s Degree in Operations Research from the Massachusetts Institute of Technology. He has been special advisor to the President of the European Community for five&nbsp;years and has served on the boards of Telecom Italia S.p.A, Finmeccanica
S.p.A. and Alitalia S.p.A. Currently, he is also a director of LandiRenzo S.p.A and Almaviva S.p.A. Mr.&nbsp;Ovi is a Life Trustee in Carnegie Mellon University and a member of the board in the Italian Institute of Technology. Until April&nbsp;2000,
he was the Chief Executive Officer of Tecnitel S.p.A., a subsidiary of Telecom Italia Group. Prior to joining Tecnitel S.p.A., Mr.&nbsp;Ovi was the Senior Vice President of International Affairs and Communications at I.R.I. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Alessandro Rivera has been a member of our Supervisory Board since May 2011. Mr. Rivera serves on our Supervisory
Board&#146;s Compensation Committee and Nominating and Corporate Governance Committee. He has been the Head of Directorate IV &#147;Financial Sector Policy and Regulation Legal Affairs&#148; at the Department of the Treasury, Ministry of Economy and
Finance, since 2008. He served as Head of Unit in the Department of the Treasury from 2000 to 2008 and was responsible for a variety of policy matters: financial services and markets, </FONT></P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="center"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">63 </FONT></P>



<p Style='page-break-before:always'>
<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">
banking foundations, accounting, finance, corporate governance and auditing. Since 2008, Mr. Rivera has been the Government representative in the &#147;Consiglio Superiore&#148; of the Bank of
Italy, and in the Financial Services Committee. Since 2013 he has been a member of the Board of Directors and Compensation Committee of Cassa Depositi e Prestiti. From 2011 to 2014 he was a member of the Board of Directors and Compensation Committee
of Poste Italiane S.p.A.. From 2008 to 2011 he was a member of the European Securities Committee. He was a member of the Accounting Regulatory Committee from 2002 to 2008 and a member of the Audit Regulatory Committee from 2005 to 2008. He served on
the board of Italia Lavoro S.p.A. from 2005 to 2008 and was a member of the Audit Committee and the Compensation Committee. Mr. Rivera was also the Chairman of the Audit Committee of the &#147;Fondo nazionale di garanzia degli intermediari
finanziari&#148; (Italian investor compensation scheme) from 2003 to 2008. From 2001 to 2010, he was the Project Leader and Deputy Project Leader in several twinning projects with Eastern European Countries (the Russian Federation, the Czech
Republic, Lithuania, and Bulgaria). He also served on the board of Mediocredito del Friuli &#151; Venezia Giulia S.p.A from 2001 to 2003. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Martine Verluyten has been a member of our Supervisory Board since May&nbsp;2012. Ms.&nbsp;Verluyten serves on our Supervisory Board&#146;s Audit Committee and has been its Chair since April&nbsp;22,
2013. Until 2011,&nbsp;Ms.&nbsp;Verluyten acted as CFO of Umicore N.V. based in Brussels. Previously she was CFO of Mobistar N.V. <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">(2001-2006),</FONT> having initially joined Mobistar in 2000 as Group Controller. She
had earlier worked at Raychem since 1976, holding various management positions during her 23&nbsp;year tenure, from Manager European Consolidations <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">(1976-1979),</FONT> to General Accounting Manager based in the US <FONT
STYLE="white-space:nowrap">(1979-1983).</FONT> She was then promoted to Division Controller Telecom Division Europe from 1983 to 1990. In 1990, she was appointed Finance&nbsp;&amp;&nbsp;Administration Director back in Europe, then in 1995, Europe
Controller Finance&nbsp;&amp;&nbsp;Administration Director until 1999. Ms.&nbsp;Verluyten is also member of the board of directors of Thomas Cook plc, 3i plc and GBL (group Bruxelles Lambert). Ms.&nbsp;Verluyten began her career in 1973 at KPMG as
an Auditor. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B><I>Supervisory Board Committees </I></B></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><I>Membership and Attendance</I>. As of December&nbsp;31, 2014, the composition of the four standing committees of our
Supervisory Board was as follows: (i)&nbsp;Ms.&nbsp;Martine Verluyten is the Chair of the Audit Committee, and Ms.&nbsp;Janet&nbsp;G.&nbsp;Davidson, Ms.&nbsp;Heleen Kersten and Messrs. <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">Jean-Georges</FONT> Malcor and
Alessandro Ovi are members of the Audit Committee; (ii)&nbsp;Mr.&nbsp;Maurizio Tamagnini is the Chairman of the Compensation Committee, and Mr.&nbsp;Jean d&#146;Arthuys, Ms.&nbsp;Heleen Kersten, Messrs. Didier Lombard and Alessandro Rivera are
members of the Compensation Committee; (iii)&nbsp;Ms.&nbsp;Heleen Kersten is the Chair of the Nominating and Corporate Governance Committee, and Messrs.&nbsp;Jean d&#146;Arthuys, Didier Lombard, Alessandro Rivera and Maurizio Tamagnini are members
of the Nominating and Corporate Governance Committee; and (iv)&nbsp;Mr.&nbsp;Maurizio Tamagnini is the Chairman of the Strategic Committee, and Ms.&nbsp;Janet G. Davidson and Messrs. Jean d&#146;Arthuys, Didier Lombard and Alessandro Ovi are members
of the Strategic Committee. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Detailed information on attendance at full Supervisory Board and Supervisory
Board Committee meetings during 2014 is as follows: </FONT></P> <P STYLE="font-size:12px;margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px">&nbsp;</P>
